nodes.label: Large Language Model (LLM)
nodes.description: A neural network trained on massive text corpora to predict the next token in a sequence. LLMs can generate code, answer questions, reason about problems, and follow instructions. They range from 1B to 1T+ parameters. Running them locally means the model weights and computation stay on your machine — no data leaves, no API costs, no rate limits. The tradeoff is hardware requirements: a 70B model at 16-bit precision needs ~140GB of memory.

nodes.label: Transformer Architecture
nodes.description: The neural network architecture behind all modern LLMs, introduced in the 2017 'Attention Is All You Need' paper. Key innovation: self-attention lets every token attend to every other token, enabling the model to capture long-range dependencies. The architecture consists of stacked layers, each with multi-head attention and feed-forward networks. Model size is measured in parameters — the learnable weights in these layers.

nodes.label: Parameters
nodes.description: The learnable weights in a neural network. A '70B model' has 70 billion parameters. Each parameter is typically stored as a 16-bit floating point number (2 bytes), so a 70B model needs ~140GB in full precision. Quantization reduces bits per parameter (e.g., 4-bit = 0.5 bytes), cutting memory requirements dramatically: that 70B model drops to ~42GB at 4-bit.

nodes.label: Quantization
nodes.description: The process of reducing the precision of model weights from high-precision (FP16/FP32) to lower-precision (INT8, INT4, FP8, FP4). This shrinks memory usage and speeds up inference at the cost of some quality. Modern quantization methods (GGUF K-quants, AWQ, GPTQ) lose remarkably little quality — a 4-bit model typically retains 90-95% of the full-precision quality. The key tradeoff: bits per weight vs quality vs speed.

nodes.label: Inference
nodes.description: Running a trained model to generate output (as opposed to training it). Local LLM inference has two phases: prefill (processing the input prompt, compute-bound) and decode (generating tokens one by one, memory-bandwidth-bound). Decode speed is measured in tokens per second (tok/s). On consumer hardware, expect 5-30 tok/s depending on model size and quantization.

nodes.label: Context Window
nodes.description: The maximum number of tokens a model can process in a single request — input plus output. Measured in tokens (roughly 0.75 words each). Modern models range from 8K to 256K+ tokens. Longer context requires more memory for the KV cache (key-value pairs stored during attention). On 128GB hardware, a 70B model at 4-bit can practically use ~80-100K tokens before memory runs out.

nodes.label: KV Cache
nodes.description: During inference, the model stores key and value tensors from its attention layers for all previously processed tokens. This avoids recomputing them for each new token but grows linearly with context length. A 70B model at 128K context can use 20-40GB just for KV cache. KV cache quantization (Q4/Q8 KV in llama.cpp) dramatically reduces this overhead — a key optimization for long-context local inference.

nodes.label: Mixture of Experts (MoE)
nodes.description: An architecture where only a subset of parameters (experts) are active for each token. Mixtral 8x22B has 141B total but only 39B active per token; Qwen3-Coder-Next has 80B total but only 3B active. MoE models give better quality per compute, but ALL parameters must fit in memory even though only some are used. Unified memory (like Strix Halo) is ideal — no PCIe bottleneck for accessing the dormant experts.

nodes.label: GPU (Graphics Processing Unit)
nodes.description: Massively parallel processors originally for graphics, now essential for AI. GPUs excel at matrix multiplication — the core operation in transformer inference. Key specs: VRAM (how much model fits), memory bandwidth (determines tok/s), and compute (TFLOPS, determines prefill speed). Discrete GPUs (NVIDIA RTX, AMD Radeon) have dedicated VRAM; integrated GPUs (like Strix Halo) share system memory.

nodes.label: VRAM / Unified Memory
nodes.description: The memory available to the GPU for storing model weights and KV cache. Discrete GPUs have dedicated VRAM (typically 8-48GB). Unified memory architectures (Apple Silicon, AMD Strix Halo) share system RAM between CPU and GPU — giving access to 64-128GB+ without PCIe transfer bottlenecks. This is the single most important spec for local LLMs: it determines the largest model you can run.

nodes.label: Memory Bandwidth
nodes.description: The rate at which data can be read from memory, measured in GB/s. This is the bottleneck for token generation (decode phase): each token requires reading all model weights once. A 70B model at 4-bit (42GB) on Strix Halo (~215 GB/s) generates ~5 tok/s (215/42). Discrete GPUs have higher bandwidth (NVIDIA RTX 4090: ~1TB/s) but less memory (24GB). The unified memory tradeoff: more capacity, lower bandwidth.

nodes.label: CUDA
nodes.description: NVIDIA's proprietary parallel computing platform. The dominant ecosystem for AI/ML. Most inference engines (vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, ExLlamaV2) are CUDA-first. Tensor Cores provide hardware-accelerated matrix multiplication in FP16/INT8/FP8/FP4. Not available on AMD hardware — AMD users use ROCm or Vulkan instead.

nodes.label: NPU (Neural Processing Unit)
nodes.description: Dedicated AI accelerator built into modern CPUs/SoCs. Optimized for neural network operations at low power. AMD's XDNA 2 in Strix Halo provides 50 TOPS. NPUs are efficient for smaller models and prompt prefill, but lack the memory bandwidth of GPUs for large-model token generation. Still early days for LLM inference on NPUs — Lemonade and FastFlowLM are pioneering this space.

nodes.label: Tool Calling / Function Calling
nodes.description: The ability of an LLM to invoke external tools (read files, run commands, search code) during a conversation. Critical for coding assistants like Claude Code. The model outputs structured JSON describing which function to call and with what arguments. Not all local models support it reliably — tool calling requires strong instruction following and correct JSON generation. Qwen3-Coder-Next and Devstral 2 have the best local tool calling support.

nodes.label: Tokens
nodes.description: The basic units of text that LLMs process. Text is split into tokens by a tokenizer — roughly 1 token per 0.75 English words, though code is often denser (more tokens per line). A 128K context window means 128,000 tokens. Inference speed is measured in tokens per second (tok/s). API pricing is per token (input and output counted separately).

nodes.label: Fine-Tuning
nodes.description: Adapting a pre-trained model to a specific task or domain by training it further on curated data. DeepSeek-R1 distills are fine-tuned versions of Llama/Qwen with reasoning traces. LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is the most common method — it trains only a small number of additional parameters, making it feasible on consumer hardware. Unsloth provides optimized fine-tuning tools.

nodes.label: AMD Strix Halo
nodes.description: AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 APU with 16 Zen 5 cores, 40 RDNA 3.5 CUs (Radeon 8060S iGPU), and XDNA 2 NPU at 50 TOPS. Supports up to 128GB LPDDR5X-8000 unified memory at ~215 GB/s. The unified memory architecture means GPU and CPU share the same pool — models loaded in RAM are directly accessible by GPU compute units, enabling 70B+ parameter models that discrete GPUs cannot fit.

nodes.label: Vulkan Backend
nodes.description: GPU compute API via the RADV open-source driver. Currently faster than ROCm on Strix Halo for both prompt processing and token generation, and much easier to set up. The recommended backend for llama.cpp on Strix Halo as of early 2026.

nodes.label: ROCm (HIP)
nodes.description: AMD's open GPU compute platform. ROCm 7.x has performance regressions on Strix Halo — HIP barely outperforms CPU for prompt processing, with regressions at high context sizes. Workaround: compiler flag -mllvm --amdgpu-unroll-threshold-local=600. KV cache may get dumped to shared memory instead of VRAM. Flash attention (-fa 1) and --no-mmap are mandatory for stable usage.

nodes.label: XDNA 2 NPU
nodes.description: AMD's neural processing unit at 50 TOPS on Strix Halo. Can handle prompt processing (prefill) for fast time-to-first-token while the iGPU handles token generation (decode). Model support is still limited compared to GPU; the NPU does not have access to the full memory bandwidth. Best for power-efficient inference of smaller models.

nodes.label: llama.cpp
nodes.description: The foundational C/C++ inference engine for running LLMs on consumer hardware. Supports GGUF format, all quantization levels, multiple backends (CUDA, ROCm, Vulkan, Metal, CPU). Features KV cache quantization (Q4/Q8) that dramatically reduces context memory overhead. Includes llama-server for OpenAI-compatible HTTP API. The standard against which all other local inference is measured.

nodes.label: Ollama
nodes.description: User-friendly wrapper around llama.cpp with a CLI and REST API. Since v0.14.0 (January 2026), natively supports the Anthropic Messages API — enabling direct connection from Claude Code without a translation proxy. Model library is curated but extensive. Supports AMD GPUs via ROCm and Vulkan. The simplest path to running local models.

nodes.label: vLLM
nodes.description: High-throughput serving engine optimized for batched workloads. Supports PagedAttention for efficient KV cache management, AWQ and GPTQ quantization, continuous batching. OpenAI-compatible API. AMD ROCm support exists but less mature than llama.cpp. Better suited for multi-user server scenarios than single-user desktop inference.

nodes.label: LM Studio
nodes.description: Desktop GUI application built on llama.cpp. Provides easy model browsing and downloading from HuggingFace, one-click inference, and an OpenAI-compatible local server. Good for experimentation and model evaluation before committing to a production setup.

nodes.label: Lemonade Server
nodes.description: AMD's lightweight native C++ inference service (2MB binary). Exposes OpenAI-compatible API at localhost:13305. Splits workload between NPU (prefill for fast time-to-first-token) and iGPU (decode for bandwidth). Purpose-built for AMD AI PCs including Strix Halo.

nodes.label: FastFlowLM
nodes.description: Purpose-built inference runtime for AMD NPUs, described as 'like Ollama but for AMD NPUs'. Focuses on leveraging the XDNA NPU for efficient local inference on AMD AI PCs.

nodes.label: ExLlamaV2
nodes.description: GPU-focused inference engine with flexible per-layer quantization via EXL2 format. Achieves excellent token throughput on NVIDIA GPUs. Less relevant for AMD setups but worth noting for its innovative quantization approach.

nodes.label: SGLang
nodes.description: Structured generation language and runtime. Excels at constrained decoding (JSON schemas, regex patterns). Supports CUDA and ROCm. Competitive with vLLM for serving workloads, with better structured output performance.

nodes.label: llamafile
nodes.description: Single-file executable that bundles a model with the inference engine (based on llama.cpp + Cosmopolitan libc). Runs on any x86-64 or ARM64 OS without installation. Great for distribution and air-gapped deployments.

nodes.label: GGUF
nodes.description: The standard quantization format for llama.cpp and Ollama. Successor to GGML. Supports K-quants (Q2_K through Q8_0), I-quants (IQ1_S through IQ4_XS), and full precision (F16, F32). K-quants offer the best quality-per-bit tradeoff for most use cases. GGUF files are self-contained with model metadata, tokenizer, and weights in a single file.

nodes.label: GPTQ
nodes.description: Post-training quantization method that uses approximate second-order information for weight compression. Typically INT4 or INT8. Requires calibration data. Supported by vLLM, HuggingFace Transformers, AutoGPTQ. More GPU-centric than GGUF — optimized for CUDA tensor cores.

nodes.label: AWQ
nodes.description: Activation-aware Weight Quantization. Preserves salient weight channels identified by activation magnitudes. Typically INT4. Supported by vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, HuggingFace. Often slightly better quality than GPTQ at same bit width for instruction-following tasks.

nodes.label: EXL2
nodes.description: ExLlamaV2's per-layer mixed-precision format. Allows different quantization bits per layer based on sensitivity analysis. Achieves very good quality at low average bits-per-weight. Exclusive to ExLlamaV2 engine.

nodes.label: FP8
nodes.description: 8-bit floating point format (E4M3 or E5M2). Supported natively on NVIDIA Hopper (H100) and Blackwell GPUs. Near-lossless quality compared to FP16. Halves memory vs FP16. Supported by vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang.

nodes.label: LiteLLM
nodes.description: Open-source proxy that translates between API formats. Sits between Claude Code and local/remote model servers. Provides load balancing, fallbacks, cost tracking, and model routing. Translates Anthropic Messages API to OpenAI-compatible format for backends that don't speak Anthropic natively. Security note: PyPI versions 1.82.7 and 1.82.8 were compromised with credential-stealing malware.

nodes.label: CCProxy
nodes.description: Go-based proxy specifically designed for Claude Code. Intercepts and routes requests with intelligent model routing based on token count, tool usage, or custom rules. Can connect Claude Code to Ollama, OpenAI, GitHub Copilot, or Claude Pro/Max via OAuth.

nodes.label: Bifrost
nodes.description: Lightweight API gateway for routing AI model requests. One of five commonly used gateways alongside LiteLLM, Cloudflare AI Gateway, Kong AI Gateway, and OpenRouter.

nodes.label: Anthropic Messages API
nodes.description: The /v1/messages endpoint format that Claude Code speaks natively. Request/response format includes system prompts, multi-turn messages, tool definitions, and streaming. Ollama v0.14.0+ supports this natively. LiteLLM and CCProxy can translate from this to other formats.

nodes.label: OpenAI-Compatible API
nodes.description: The /v1/chat/completions endpoint format spoken by most local inference engines (llama.cpp server, vLLM, LM Studio, Lemonade). De facto standard for local model serving. Requires translation layer (LiteLLM, CCProxy) to work with Claude Code.

nodes.label: Claude Code
nodes.description: Anthropic's official CLI coding assistant. Speaks the Anthropic Messages API. Can be pointed at local models via ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL. Sends a ~16k token system prompt, so local models need 64k+ context. Supports /model for mid-session switching. Tool calling is the main pain point with local models — some engines drop tool call responses during streaming.

nodes.label: Aider
nodes.description: CLI coding assistant with deep git integration. Supports any OpenAI-compatible endpoint, making it trivially compatible with local models. Features multi-file editing, auto-commits, repository mapping. Strong community and active development. Excellent alternative to Claude Code for local-only workflows.

nodes.label: Continue.dev
nodes.description: VS Code and JetBrains plugin with model-agnostic architecture (~31k GitHub stars). Connects to any model via Ollama, LM Studio, or OpenAI-compatible API. Provides chat, autocomplete, and inline editing. Good for IDE-integrated workflows alongside Claude Code in the terminal.

nodes.label: Tabby
nodes.description: Self-hosted code completion server (~33k GitHub stars). Runs entirely on your infrastructure with full data governance. Team-oriented with repository indexing. Good for fast inline completions powered by smaller local models while Claude Code handles complex tasks.

nodes.label: OpenHands
nodes.description: Leading open-source coding agent framework (64k+ GitHub stars, formerly OpenDevin). Works with any LLM including local models via Ollama. AMD partnership with Lemonade integration for AI PC inference. Open-weight models are within 2.2-6.4% of proprietary models on their benchmarks. Web UI, multi-agent, production-ready.

nodes.label: Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B
nodes.description: Alibaba's flagship open code model. 32B parameters, 128K context. Matches or exceeds GPT-4o on HumanEval (~92%), MBPP (~85%). At Q8_0 uses ~34GB — near-lossless quality with room for full 128K context on 128GB hardware. The sweet spot for local code generation.

nodes.label: Qwen3-Coder-Next
nodes.description: 80B MoE model with only 3B active parameters. 256K native context (1M with YaRN). Agentic-trained — comparable to Claude Sonnet on coding benchmarks. Uses ~46GB on unified memory. Excellent performance because only active parameters need bandwidth. Strong tool calling support.

nodes.label: Devstral Small 2
nodes.description: Mistral's 24B coding model. 68.0% on SWE-bench Verified. Apache 2.0 license. Runs on consumer GPUs. Designed for agentic coding workflows (plan/execute/verify). Works with Mistral's Vibe CLI.

nodes.label: Devstral 2
nodes.description: Mistral's large 123B coding model. 72.2% on SWE-bench Verified. Modified MIT license. ~75GB at Q4_K_M — fits in 128GB but leaves limited room for context. Best open-source model for complex agentic coding tasks.

nodes.label: Codestral 22B
nodes.description: Mistral's 22B code model. 32K context. HumanEval ~81%. Strong across many programming languages. ~14GB at Q4_K_M. Efficient and fast. License was initially non-commercial — verify current terms.

nodes.label: StarCoder2 15B
nodes.description: BigCode/ServiceNow's 15B code model. 16K context. Good for fill-in-the-middle (FIM) and autocomplete — the type of task Tabby and Continue.dev use for inline completions. Outclassed for chat-style coding by newer models.

nodes.label: Llama 3.3 70B
nodes.description: Meta's dense 70B model (December 2024). 128K context. Matches Llama 3.1 405B quality at 70B size. HumanEval ~82%. At Q5_K_M uses ~50GB, Q6_K ~58GB. Strong general-purpose + code capability. One of the best all-round models for 128GB hardware.

nodes.label: Qwen 2.5 72B
nodes.description: Alibaba's top-tier general model. 72B parameters, 128K context with YaRN. HumanEval ~86%. At Q4_K_M uses ~45GB, Q6_K ~60GB. Competitive with GPT-4 on many benchmarks. Excellent for both code and general reasoning.

nodes.label: Mixtral 8x22B
nodes.description: Mistral's large MoE model. 141B total, 39B active parameters. 64K context. At Q4_K_M uses ~85GB — fits in 128GB but tight. Good general capability. The MoE architecture gives better quality per active parameter than dense models.

nodes.label: Phi-4 14B
nodes.description: Microsoft's compact reasoning model. 14B parameters, 16K context. HumanEval ~82% — remarkable for its size. At Q8_0 uses only ~15GB. Excellent as a fast draft/secondary model. Can run alongside a larger model without memory pressure.

nodes.label: Gemma 2 27B
nodes.description: Google's 27B model. Punches above its weight — competitive with Llama 3.1 70B on some tasks. At Q4_K_M uses ~17GB. Limitation: only 8K context, which is restrictive for code tasks requiring large file context.

nodes.label: DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B
nodes.description: Llama 3.1 70B fine-tuned with DeepSeek R1's reasoning traces. Produces <think>...</think> blocks with chain-of-thought before answering. At Q5_K_M uses ~50GB. The strongest reasoning model that fits on 128GB hardware. Ideal for architecture decisions and complex debugging.

nodes.label: DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B
nodes.description: Qwen 2.5 32B fine-tuned with R1 reasoning traces. Matches or exceeds GPT-4o on math/reasoning tasks. At Q4_K_M uses ~20GB. The efficiency sweet spot for reasoning — fast inference, full 128K context, strong chain-of-thought.

nodes.label: QwQ-32B
nodes.description: Alibaba's reasoning model (March 2025). 32B parameters, 128K context with long chain-of-thought. Competitive with DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B on math/reasoning. At Q4_K_M uses ~20GB. Different reasoning flavor than R1 — worth comparing both.

nodes.label: HuggingFace Hub
nodes.description: Primary distribution platform for all open LLMs. All model families publish here. GGUF files hosted directly. Search for model name + 'GGUF' to find quantized versions. Has more quantization variety than the Ollama library.

nodes.label: bartowski
nodes.description: Currently the most active GGUF quantizer on HuggingFace. Covers nearly every new model release within hours. Provides multiple quant levels per model. Experiments with imatrix calibration and has contributed to llama.cpp quantization recipes.

nodes.label: Unsloth
nodes.description: Known for Dynamic 2.0 GGUFs — an improved quantization methodology using calibration datasets. Also provides fine-tuning tools. Active competitor to bartowski. Releases 25+ GGUF variants per major model. Dynamic 2.0 works on all models including MoEs.

nodes.label: LocalAI
nodes.description: Open-source Go-based AI engine. Drop-in replacement for both OpenAI AND Anthropic APIs natively (since v3.10.0). Supports multiple backends: llama.cpp, vLLM, Transformers, ExLlama2. Handles text, image, audio, video, embeddings, TTS, function calling, and constrained grammars. The only engine besides Ollama that speaks Anthropic Messages API natively.

nodes.label: kobold.cpp
nodes.description: Single-file llama.cpp fork with integrated web UI. Focused on creative writing and roleplay but fully capable for code. Triple API: KoboldAI + OpenAI + Ollama compatible endpoints. Zero-install single executable with built-in memory and SillyTavern integration.

nodes.label: Aphrodite Engine
nodes.description: vLLM-based engine by PygmalionAI with the broadest quantization support of any engine: AQLM, AWQ, BitNet, bitsandbytes, EETQ, GGUF, GPTQ, QuIP#, EXL2, Marlin, FP2-FP12, NVFP4, MXFP4, and more. OpenAI-compatible API. Context parallelism and dynamic model management. v0.10.0 (Nov 2025).

nodes.label: PowerInfer
nodes.description: Sparse heterogeneous GPU-CPU inference engine from SJTU-IPADS. Exploits power-law neuron activation: hot neurons on GPU, cold neurons on CPU. Up to 11.69x faster than llama.cpp. 13.20 tok/s on OPT-175B with single RTX 4090 (only 18% below A100). PowerInfer-2 targets smartphones.

nodes.label: MLC LLM
nodes.description: Machine learning compiler for universal LLM deployment via Apache TVM. Compiles models to run on CUDA, Vulkan, Metal, WebGPU (in-browser via WebLLM), iOS, Android. ML compilation optimizes per-target. WebLLM enables full in-browser inference with no server.

nodes.label: EXL3
nodes.description: ExLlamaV3's next-gen format using QTIP-based Hadamard transforms and trellis encoding on 16x16 Tensor Core tiles. Coherent at 1.6 bits per weight — a 70B model in under 16GB VRAM. Marlin-inspired GEMM kernel approaches memory bandwidth limit at 4 bpw. NVIDIA CUDA only.

nodes.label: AQLM
nodes.description: Additive Quantization of Language Models. Multi-codebook approach dividing weight vectors into sub-vectors approximated by codewords. State-of-the-art quality at 2 bits per weight. Outperforms GPTQ and QuIP# at extreme compression levels.

nodes.label: Ollama Library
nodes.description: Curated model registry with standardized tags (e.g., qwen2.5-coder:32b-instruct-q5_K_M). One-command downloads: 'ollama pull model:tag'. Not every quantization is available — HuggingFace has more variety. But the simplest way to get started.

edges.label: speaks natively
edges.description: Claude Code uses the Anthropic Messages API (/v1/messages) for all communication. This is the only protocol it understands.

edges.label: supports since v0.14
edges.description: Since January 2026, Ollama natively serves the Anthropic Messages API, enabling direct connection from Claude Code without a translation proxy.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: Ollama also serves the OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions endpoint, making it compatible with most coding assistants.

edges.label: wraps
edges.description: Ollama uses llama.cpp as its inference backend, adding model management, a REST API, and a curated model library on top.

edges.label: serves via llama-server
edges.description: llama-server provides an OpenAI-compatible HTTP API for any GGUF model loaded by llama.cpp.

edges.label: uses
edges.description: GGUF is the native and only format supported by llama.cpp. All quantization levels from Q2_K to F16 are available.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: Vulkan is currently the fastest backend for llama.cpp on Strix Halo, outperforming ROCm for both prompt processing and token generation.

edges.label: supports (with issues)
edges.description: ROCm/HIP backend works but has performance regressions on Strix Halo. Requires workarounds: flash attention, --no-mmap, compiler flags.

edges.label: serves
edges.description: vLLM provides an OpenAI-compatible API. Better for batched multi-user serving than single-user desktop inference.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: vLLM supports AWQ quantized models for efficient GPU inference.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: vLLM supports GPTQ quantized models.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: vLLM supports FP8 inference on compatible hardware (NVIDIA Hopper/Blackwell).

edges.label: built on
edges.description: LM Studio uses llama.cpp as its inference backend, providing a GUI and model browser on top.

edges.label: serves
edges.description: LM Studio can expose a local OpenAI-compatible server for other tools to connect to.

edges.label: serves
edges.description: Lemonade exposes an OpenAI-compatible API at localhost:13305.

edges.label: leverages for prefill
edges.description: Lemonade uses the XDNA NPU for prompt processing (prefill) to achieve fast time-to-first-token.

edges.label: optimized for
edges.description: Lemonade is AMD's purpose-built inference server for AI PCs including Strix Halo, splitting work between NPU and iGPU.

edges.label: built for
edges.description: FastFlowLM is specifically designed to leverage AMD's XDNA NPU for local inference.

edges.label: uses
edges.description: EXL2 is ExLlamaV2's native format with per-layer mixed-precision quantization.

edges.label: serves
edges.description: SGLang provides an OpenAI-compatible API with excellent structured generation support.

edges.label: translates from
edges.description: LiteLLM accepts Anthropic Messages API requests from Claude Code and translates them to whatever format the backend model server speaks.

edges.label: translates to
edges.description: LiteLLM forwards requests to OpenAI-compatible backends (llama.cpp, vLLM, LM Studio) after format translation.

edges.label: intercepts
edges.description: CCProxy sits between Claude Code and model servers, intercepting Anthropic API requests for intelligent routing based on token count, tool usage, or custom rules.

edges.label: routes to
edges.description: CCProxy can route Claude Code requests to a local Ollama instance for simple tasks.

edges.label: connects via ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL
edges.description: Set ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL to point at LiteLLM proxy. Claude Code sends all requests there; LiteLLM handles routing and translation.

edges.label: connects via ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL
edges.description: CCProxy provides intelligent routing — sending complex tasks to cloud Anthropic and simple tasks to local models.

edges.label: direct connection (v0.14+)
edges.description: With Ollama's native Anthropic API support, Claude Code can connect directly without any proxy. Set ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL to localhost:11434.

edges.label: speaks natively
edges.description: Aider connects to any OpenAI-compatible endpoint, making it trivially compatible with all local inference engines.

edges.label: connects to
edges.description: Continue.dev has first-class Ollama integration for chat, autocomplete, and inline editing in VS Code/JetBrains.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: Continue.dev can connect to any OpenAI-compatible server.

edges.label: commonly uses
edges.description: Tabby is often paired with StarCoder2 or similar FIM-capable models for fast inline code completions.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: OpenHands works with local models via Ollama. AMD partnership integrates it with Lemonade for AI PC inference.

edges.label: AMD integration
edges.description: AMD partnered with OpenHands to integrate Lemonade for local AI PC inference, bringing agent capabilities to consumer hardware.

edges.label: best via RADV driver
edges.description: Strix Halo's RDNA 3.5 iGPU works best with the Vulkan backend via the open-source RADV driver.

edges.label: supported (regressions)
edges.description: ROCm 7.x works on Strix Halo but has performance regressions. Vulkan is currently recommended instead.

edges.label: includes
edges.description: Strix Halo includes the XDNA 2 NPU at 50 TOPS for AI acceleration.

edges.label: pulls from
edges.description: Ollama's 'ollama pull' command downloads models from the curated Ollama Library.

edges.label: hosts
edges.description: HuggingFace hosts GGUF files directly. Search for model name + 'GGUF' to find quantized versions from various quantizers.

edges.label: publishes to
edges.description: bartowski publishes GGUF quantizations to HuggingFace, covering most new model releases within hours.

edges.label: produces
edges.description: bartowski is the most prolific GGUF quantizer, providing multiple quant levels with imatrix calibration.

edges.label: publishes to
edges.description: Unsloth publishes Dynamic 2.0 GGUFs to HuggingFace with 25+ variants per major model.

edges.label: produces (Dynamic 2.0)
edges.description: Unsloth's Dynamic 2.0 methodology uses calibration datasets for improved quantization quality on all model architectures.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B is available in GGUF at all quant levels. Q8_0 (~34GB) is the sweet spot for 128GB hardware.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: Qwen3-Coder-Next is available in GGUF. As a MoE model (~46GB), it benefits from unified memory architecture.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: Llama 3.3 70B is available in GGUF. Q5_K_M (~50GB) or Q6_K (~58GB) are recommended for 128GB hardware.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: Qwen 2.5 72B is available in GGUF. Q4_K_M (~45GB) to Q6_K (~60GB) fits well on 128GB hardware.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: R1-Distill-Llama-70B is available in GGUF. Q5_K_M (~50GB) is the premium choice for reasoning on 128GB hardware.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: R1-Distill-Qwen-32B at Q4_K_M (~20GB) is the efficiency sweet spot for reasoning tasks.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: QwQ-32B at Q4_K_M (~20GB) provides an alternative reasoning approach to DeepSeek R1 distills.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: Phi-4 14B at Q8_0 (~15GB) is an excellent fast secondary model that can run alongside larger models.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: Devstral Small 2 24B runs comfortably on consumer hardware in GGUF format.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: Devstral 2 123B at Q4_K_M (~75GB) fits in 128GB but leaves limited room for context.

edges.label: bundles
edges.description: llamafile embeds llama.cpp plus Cosmopolitan libc into a single executable that runs on any OS without installation.

edges.label: uses
edges.description: llamafile packages GGUF models into self-contained executables for zero-dependency distribution.

edges.label: translates from
edges.description: Bifrost can accept Anthropic API requests and route them to various backends, similar to LiteLLM but lighter weight.

edges.label: translates to
edges.description: Bifrost forwards translated requests to OpenAI-compatible backends.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: Codestral 22B at Q4_K_M (~14GB) is efficient and fast — a good secondary code model.

edges.label: evolved into
edges.description: Codestral's architecture was the foundation for the Devstral family of agentic coding models.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: Mixtral 8x22B at Q4_K_M (~85GB) fits in 128GB but tight — limited room for long context.

edges.label: benefits from unified memory
edges.description: MoE models like Mixtral 8x22B benefit greatly from unified memory — all 141B parameters are accessible without PCIe transfers.

edges.label: available as
edges.description: Gemma 2 27B at Q4_K_M (~17GB) is very efficient but limited by 8K context.

edges.label: available via
edges.description: Gemma 2 27B is available in the Ollama Library with multiple quantization options.

edges.label: supports natively
edges.description: Since v3.10.0, LocalAI natively serves the Anthropic Messages API alongside OpenAI — one of only two engines (with Ollama) that speak Anthropic directly.

edges.label: supports natively
edges.description: LocalAI serves OpenAI-compatible endpoints as its primary API.

edges.label: uses as backend
edges.description: LocalAI can use llama.cpp as one of its inference backends, alongside vLLM, Transformers, and ExLlama2.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: LocalAI supports GGUF, GGML, Safetensors, PyTorch, GPTQ, and AWQ formats.

edges.label: direct connection
edges.description: LocalAI speaks Anthropic Messages API natively — Claude Code can connect directly via ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL without any translation proxy.

edges.label: forks
edges.description: kobold.cpp is a fork of llama.cpp with an integrated web UI and triple API compatibility (KoboldAI + OpenAI + Ollama).

edges.label: uses
edges.description: kobold.cpp uses GGUF format, inheriting all quantization levels from llama.cpp.

edges.label: serves
edges.description: kobold.cpp serves KoboldAI, OpenAI, and Ollama compatible endpoints simultaneously.

edges.label: forks
edges.description: Aphrodite is based on vLLM with extended quantization support — adding AQLM, QuIP#, EXL2, BitNet, and FP2-FP12 formats.

edges.label: serves
edges.description: Aphrodite provides an OpenAI-compatible API with the broadest quantization format support of any engine.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: Aphrodite is one of the few engines supporting AQLM for extreme 2-bit quantization.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: Aphrodite supports GGUF alongside its native safetensors-based formats.

edges.label: forks
edges.description: PowerInfer is a llama.cpp fork that adds sparse heterogeneous GPU-CPU inference via neuron activation prediction.

edges.label: benefits from unified memory
edges.description: PowerInfer's GPU-CPU hybrid approach is especially effective on unified memory where hot/cold neuron placement has zero transfer overhead.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: MLC LLM compiles models to Vulkan for cross-platform GPU inference, relevant for Strix Halo's RDNA 3.5.

edges.label: successor format for
edges.description: EXL3 is the next-generation quantization format from turboderp, replacing EXL2 with QTIP-based trellis encoding for dramatically better quality at low bit widths.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: LM Studio supports Vulkan offloading, outperforming Ollama on integrated GPUs like Strix Halo's RDNA 3.5.

edges.label: supports
edges.description: SGLang supports AMD ROCm for GPU inference, including MI300X/MI325X data center GPUs.

edges.label: built on
edges.description: All modern LLMs use the transformer architecture, which enables parallel processing of tokens through self-attention.

edges.label: sized by
edges.description: Model capability roughly scales with parameter count. A 70B model is generally more capable than a 7B model, but also needs 10x the memory.

edges.label: processes
edges.description: LLMs read and generate text as tokens — sub-word units produced by a tokenizer. Everything is measured in tokens: context length, speed, cost.

edges.label: runs via
edges.description: Inference is the act of running a trained model to generate output. Local inference means the computation happens on your hardware.

edges.label: requires
edges.description: During inference, the KV cache stores attention state for all processed tokens. It grows linearly with context length and can consume significant memory.

edges.label: bottlenecked by
edges.description: Token generation speed is determined by memory bandwidth — each new token requires reading all model weights from memory once.

edges.label: compresses
edges.description: Quantization reduces the bits used to store each parameter: from 16-bit (2 bytes) down to 4-bit (0.5 bytes) or lower, shrinking memory requirements proportionally.

edges.label: implements
edges.description: GGUF is a file format that packages quantized model weights. K-quants (Q2_K to Q8_0) and I-quants are the specific quantization methods it supports.

edges.label: implements
edges.description: GPTQ uses approximate second-order information from calibration data to optimize weight quantization with minimal quality loss.

edges.label: implements
edges.description: AWQ identifies and preserves salient weight channels by observing activation patterns during calibration.

edges.label: measured in
edges.description: Context windows are measured in tokens. A 128K context model can process ~96,000 words of input+output in a single request.

edges.label: consumes via
edges.description: Longer context means a larger KV cache. At 128K tokens, a 70B model's KV cache can use 20-40GB of memory.

edges.label: has
edges.description: GPUs have dedicated or shared memory for storing model weights and KV cache. Discrete GPUs: 8-48GB VRAM. Unified memory: up to 128GB+.

edges.label: provides
edges.description: GPU memory bandwidth determines token generation speed. Discrete: 500-1000+ GB/s. Unified (Strix Halo): ~215 GB/s.

edges.label: NVIDIA uses
edges.description: NVIDIA GPUs use CUDA for compute. Most AI inference engines are CUDA-first, giving NVIDIA a software ecosystem advantage.

edges.label: 128GB unified
edges.description: Strix Halo's 128GB LPDDR5X is unified memory — shared between CPU, GPU, and NPU. The key advantage over discrete GPUs for large models.

edges.label: ~215 GB/s
edges.description: Strix Halo achieves ~215 GB/s real-world memory bandwidth. Lower than discrete GPUs but adequate for 5+ tok/s on 70B models.

edges.label: integrates
edges.description: Strix Halo integrates an RDNA 3.5 iGPU with 40 compute units — a GPU that shares the system's unified memory pool.

edges.label: is a
edges.description: AMD's XDNA 2 is an NPU — a dedicated neural processing unit optimized for AI workloads at low power.

edges.label: has total vs active
edges.description: MoE models have many total parameters but activate only a subset per token. Mixtral 8x22B: 141B total, 39B active. More parameters in memory, less compute per token.

edges.label: needs full size in
edges.description: All MoE parameters must fit in memory even though only a fraction are active per token. Unified memory is ideal — no transfer overhead for dormant experts.

edges.label: uses
edges.description: Mixtral 8x22B is an 8-expert MoE model: 141B total parameters, 39B active per token.

edges.label: uses
edges.description: Qwen3-Coder-Next is an MoE model with 80B total but only 3B active parameters — excellent efficiency on unified memory.

edges.label: extends
edges.description: Tool calling gives LLMs the ability to invoke external functions during a conversation, transforming them from text generators into agents.

edges.label: requires
edges.description: Claude Code relies heavily on tool calling — reading files, running commands, editing code. Local models must support it reliably to work with Claude Code.

edges.label: adapts
edges.description: Fine-tuning takes a pre-trained LLM and trains it further on specific data. This creates specialized models like code assistants or reasoning models.

edges.label: created via
edges.description: R1-Distill models are Llama/Qwen base models fine-tuned on DeepSeek R1's chain-of-thought reasoning traces.

edges.label: provides tools for
edges.description: Unsloth offers optimized fine-tuning tools alongside their GGUF quantizations — enabling users to create custom model variants.

edges.label: GPU compute API
edges.description: Vulkan is a cross-platform GPU compute API. On AMD hardware without CUDA, Vulkan provides GPU-accelerated inference via the RADV driver.

edges.label: AMD GPU compute
edges.description: ROCm is AMD's open GPU compute platform — their answer to NVIDIA's CUDA. Supports AMD discrete and integrated GPUs.
